transcript.text: I'm exhausted from the recent Lyme flare-up and struggling to keep up with deadlines; the wedding invitation just reminds me how much I'm missing out on social events. My body feels heavy and I keep worrying about whether the symptoms will get worse, which makes me doubt if I can keep working. I keep putting off tasks because I don't want to force myself when I'm feeling so drained. I haven't slept well lately, and I feel really lonely and guilty about it all.

You're dealing with a lot right now, and it's understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed. The exhaustion from the Lyme flare-up is really taking a toll, and it's affecting not just your work but also how you're feeling about social events and your overall sense of capability. It sounds like the worry about your symptoms getting worse is a big part of what's holding you back. Can you tell me more about what's been the hardest part of dealing with these symptoms and how they're impacting your daily life?

The hardest part is that I can't predict when the flare‑ups will strike, and it feels like I'm losing my identity as a designer. Deadlines loom like a wall and I keep putting them off because I don't want to push myself. I also feel guilty for missing the wedding and I barely sleep, which makes me feel even more alone.

The deadlines are feeling like a constant weight on you, and it's hard to know how to pace yourself when the flare-ups are so unpredictable. It's like your whole sense of self is tied up in your design work, and when that's impacted, everything else feels off too. You're not just dealing with the physical symptoms, but also the emotional toll of feeling like you're losing your footing in your career. What's one thing you've been putting off that's feeling particularly overwhelming right now?

The thing I’m putting off is finishing the final mockup for that eco‑friendly brand I pitched last month. I keep thinking that if I put it out there I’ll look incompetent because my body feels so weak, and I’m scared the design will reflect my illness. I feel alone working on it and worry that the flare‑up will ruin everything.

The fear of being seen as incompetent because of your illness is really holding you back, and it's understandable that you're worried about how your design will be perceived. It sounds like this project has become tied up in your feelings of self-worth, and that's a lot of pressure to put on yourself. Can you tell me more about what you think would happen if you did put the design out there, and how that would affect your sense of identity as a designer?

I keep picturing my clients seeing a design that looks rushed because I can’t focus; I worry that they'll think I'm not competent, and that would reinforce my feeling that I'm a failure as a designer. It also makes me feel alone and guilty for not delivering. I’m not sure how I could even approach it when I’m exhausted and my body feels heavy. I haven’t slept well, so my brain is fuzzy and I feel even more isolated.

You're really worried that your clients will see your design as rushed and think you're not competent, and that's feeling like a huge weight on you right now. It's like your exhaustion and physical symptoms are making it even harder to focus and feel confident in your work. I can see how that would be really tough to deal with, especially when you're already feeling isolated and guilty about not delivering. Yeah, it's a lot to handle, and it's understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed.

I keep thinking if I don't finish it, I'm just letting everyone down, but I also can't imagine how my body feels when I try to focus. Maybe I should just sleep more, but I don't know if that will help. I'm also scared that my design will look rushed and that people will see me as incompetent.

You're feeling really trapped between the pressure to finish this design project and the exhaustion that's making it hard to focus. It's like your body and mind are at odds, and that's a really tough spot to be in. The fear of being seen as incompetent is a heavy burden to carry, especially when you're already feeling guilty about not delivering. Yeah, it's understandable that you're feeling overwhelmed and unsure about how to move forward.

Honestly, I keep thinking that if I skip the wedding I can finally get some rest, but then I feel even more alone and guilty. My body feels heavy and I can barely focus, so the mockup feels like a mountain I can't climb. I worry that the design will look rushed and that my clients will see me as incompetent, which is just another layer of my health anxiety. I don't know if sleeping more will help at all.

Your body feeling heavy and your mind racing with worries about the mockup and the wedding is a lot to handle. It's like every aspect of your life is being affected by your health anxiety and the fear of not meeting expectations. I can see how sleeping more might seem like a way to escape, but it's also understandable that you're worried it won't solve everything. Yeah, it's a really tough spot to be in, feeling alone and guilty about not being able to do what you want to do.

I'm stuck staring at the blank canvas, feeling my body just too heavy to think straight, and I keep putting off the mockup because I don't want to risk a rushed design that looks worse than my symptoms. I keep worrying that my flare‑ups will make me look incompetent, and that thought makes me feel like I'm failing as a designer, which is a body‑image nightmare. Every time I think about the next flare‑up, my heart races with health anxiety. I haven't slept well, and it feels like I'm alone in this, even though I know I should be at the wedding.

Your body feeling heavy and your mind racing with worries about the mockup is a lot to handle. It's clear that the fear of being seen as incompetent is really holding you back, and it's understandable that you're worried about how your clients will perceive your work. Yeah, it's tough to feel like you're failing as a designer, especially when you're already feeling alone and guilty about not delivering. What's the smallest step you could take right now to start moving forward with the mockup, even if it's just a tiny part of it?

Honestly, I can't even bring myself to pick up the pen because my body feels like it's in a fog. The idea of starting the mockup feels like a gamble—if I get a flare‑up, I'll look even more incompetent. I'm also terrified that I won't get any rest tonight, so I keep postponing it.

You're feeling really stuck right now, and it's like your body is weighing you down, making it hard to even think about picking up the pen. The fear of a flare-up and being seen as incompetent is a heavy burden to carry. Yeah, it's understandable that you're postponing the mockup, given how you're feeling. What's the one thing you need to feel more in control of your work right now?

I think the only thing that might help me feel more in control is a tiny, concrete step—like sketching a quick thumbnail—so I know I'm making progress. But even that feels scary because my body is foggy and I worry a flare‑up will ruin it, and I just can't sleep well, which makes everything feel more overwhelming. I also feel so alone tackling this.

Taking that first step, even something small like sketching a thumbnail, can feel really daunting when your body is foggy and you're worried about a flare-up. It's like you're facing a big hurdle, and it's understandable that you're feeling scared and alone in this. What does that tiny step look like to you, and what might make it feel less overwhelming?

I’d try a tiny 5‑minute thumbnail sketch, just to get the hand moving, but the fog in my body makes me anxious about doing it right. If I set a timer and promise to stop after 5 minutes, maybe it feels less daunting. Still, the fear that a flare‑up could ruin it, and the guilt of not sleeping, makes me feel alone.

Setting a timer for just 5 minutes to get your hand moving with a thumbnail sketch is a great idea, and it's understandable that you're anxious about doing it right, especially with the fog in your body. It's like that fear of a flare-up is always lurking, making it hard to feel confident about taking that first step. What do you think would happen if you allowed yourself to just focus on those 5 minutes, without worrying about the outcome?

common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and I can see that you're feeling really stuck and overwhelmed by your health anxiety and the pressure to deliver a good design. The fear of being seen as incompetent is a major concern for you, and it's understandable that you're worried about how your clients will perceive your work. Given your recent struggles with flare-ups and sleep, it seems like your body and mind are both feeling the strain. I'm going to use a support strategy to acknowledge your feelings and concerns, and help you feel less alone in this struggle.

evidence_used.content: feeling my body just too heavy to think straight
I'm not good enough
fear of being seen as incompetent

response.final_response: Your body feeling heavy and your mind racing with worries about the mockup is a lot to handle. It's clear that the fear of being seen as incompetent is really holding you back, and it's understandable that you're worried about how your clients will perceive your work. Yeah, it's tough to feel like you're failing as a designer, especially when you're already feeling alone and guilty about not delivering. What's the smallest step you could take right now to start moving forward with the mockup, even if it's just a tiny part of it?

turn_traces.user_message: Honestly, I can't even bring myself to pick up the pen because my body feels like it's in a fog. The idea of starting the mockup feels like a gamble—if I get a flare‑up, I'll look even more incompetent. I'm also terrified that I won't get any rest tonight, so I keep postponing it.

response.reasoning: I'm a supportive companion to this person, and they need me to acknowledge their resistance without arguing, emphasizing their autonomy. Given their current state, I'll use the support strategy to create a safe space for them to express themselves. The evidence shows that they're struggling with feelings of incompetence and health anxiety, which is holding them back from starting the mockup. I'll use this information to provide a gentle and non-judgmental response. My entry point will be to acknowledge their fear of being seen as incompetent and the physical symptoms that are making it hard for them to focus.

evidence_used.content: feeling my body just too heavy to think straight
keep worrying that my flare‑ups will make me look incompetent

response.final_response: You're feeling really stuck right now, and it's like your body is weighing you down, making it hard to even think about picking up the pen. The fear of a flare-up and being seen as incompetent is a heavy burden to carry. Yeah, it's understandable that you're postponing the mockup, given how you're feeling. What's the one thing you need to feel more in control of your work right now?

turn_traces.user_message: I think the only thing that might help me feel more in control is a tiny, concrete step—like sketching a quick thumbnail—so I know I'm making progress. But even that feels scary because my body is foggy and I worry a flare‑up will ruin it, and I just can't sleep well, which makes everything feel more overwhelming. I also feel so alone tackling this.

response.reasoning: I'm here as a supportive friend, and you're expressing a lot of emotion about feeling stuck and overwhelmed with your design project. The strategies that seem most fitting right now are support and evoke, as you're looking for a way to feel more in control and are open to exploring small steps. The evidence that stands out is your mention of wanting to take a tiny, concrete step like sketching a quick thumbnail, which suggests you're looking for a sense of progress and momentum. My entry point will be to acknowledge the scariness of taking that first step, especially when your body is feeling foggy and you're worried about a flare-up.

evidence_used.content: tiny, concrete step—like sketching a quick thumbnail
recent loss of mother, diagnosis with Lyme disease
can't even bring myself to pick up the pen because my body feels like it's in a fog

response.final_response: Taking that first step, even something small like sketching a thumbnail, can feel really daunting when your body is foggy and you're worried about a flare-up. It's like you're facing a big hurdle, and it's understandable that you're feeling scared and alone in this. What does that tiny step look like to you, and what might make it feel less overwhelming?

turn_traces.user_message: I’d try a tiny 5‑minute thumbnail sketch, just to get the hand moving, but the fog in my body makes me anxious about doing it right. If I set a timer and promise to stop after 5 minutes, maybe it feels less daunting. Still, the fear that a flare‑up could ruin it, and the guilt of not sleeping, makes me feel alone.

response.reasoning: I'm here to support this person as they navigate their resistance to starting a design project, and what they need right now is help acknowledging the anxiety and guilt that's holding them back. The strategies that seem most fitting are support and evoke, as they'll allow me to reflect their concerns without pushing them to take action they're not ready for. Evidence from their recent messages suggests that they're struggling with feelings of incompetence and a fear of not meeting expectations, which is exacerbated by their physical symptoms and lack of sleep. My entry point will be to acknowledge the anxiety they're feeling about taking that first step, and to explore what might make it feel less daunting for them.

evidence_used.content: tiny, concrete step—like sketching a quick thumbnail
I'm not good enough
foggy and I worry a flare‑up will ruin it

response.final_response: Setting a timer for just 5 minutes to get your hand moving with a thumbnail sketch is a great idea, and it's understandable that you're anxious about doing it right, especially with the fog in your body. It's like that fear of a flare-up is always lurking, making it hard to feel confident about taking that first step. What do you think would happen if you allowed yourself to just focus on those 5 minutes, without worrying about the outcome?
